title: Apple galette
subtitle: A freeform rough puff tart with thinly sliced apple
excerpt: A freeform tart made by arranging thinly sliced apple over a rough puff pastry round, folding the edges loosely, and baking until the pastry is shattering and the apple is tender and lightly caramelised. No tin required.
sourceNotes: The galette in the French tradition of a freeform, folded pastry tart is documented in Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), and in Elizabeth David, French Provincial Cooking (1960). The rough puff pastry method as a home shortcut to full puff is attested in Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, and remains the standard home approach for rustic tarts in French and British baking.

content.text: is the most forgiving format in pastry: a round of dough with the edges folded up over the filling. No tin, no blind baking, no precision. The rough puff pastry here, made the same way as the pastel de nata dough, gives the galette lift and flake without the labour of proper puff. The
bakes to a deeply layered, shattering crust that makes a galette worth making from scratch rather than using shop-bought.

The filling is just apple, sugar, and a little butter on top. That plainness is the point. The pastry and the fruit carry the dish. Any extra flavour (cinnamon, almond cream, vanilla) is optional and should not crowd the apple.

attrs.prepNote: very cold, for the rough puff; plus 20 g diced cold, for the filling
peeled, cored, and sliced 3 mm thick
juice only, to toss the apple

content.text: Mix {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a bowl. Add the pastry-quantity very cold {{unsalted-butter}} in rough 1 cm pieces and toss briefly, the butter should remain in pea-sized to grape-sized chunks. Add 70 to 80 ml of ice-cold water and mix with a fork until the dough just holds together; it will be rough and ragged. On a floured surface, roll to a rough rectangle, fold in thirds, rotate 90 degrees, and roll again. Repeat twice more. Wrap and refrigerate for 60 minutes.

Heat the oven to 210°C fan (230°C conventional) and line a large baking tray with baking paper. Toss {{apple}} slices with the juice from {{lemon}} and 30 g of {{caster-sugar}}. Roll the chilled pastry on a lightly floured surface to a rough circle about 30 cm across and 3 mm thick; imperfect edges are fine. Transfer to the lined tray. Arrange the apple slices in overlapping concentric rings across the pastry, leaving a 4 cm border. Scatter the remaining {{caster-sugar}} over the apple and dot with the filling-quantity cold {{unsalted-butter}}. Fold the pastry border up and over the outer edge of the apple, pleating loosely as you go. Brush the folded pastry with beaten {{eggs}}. Bake for 28 to 32 minutes until the pastry is deeply golden and the apple is tender with some caramelised edges. Rest for 20 minutes before slicing.
A mix of eating and cooking apple works best. The eating apple holds its shape. The cooking apple breaks down and softens into the caramelised sugar.
A thin layer of almond cream (frangipane) spread on the pastry before the apple adds richness and helps the apple adhere.
Serve with crème fraîche rather than cream; the slight sourness lifts the sweetness of the caramelised fruit.
